lens.included_activities: No operating establishment in the defined U.S. NAICS population qualifies. The fixed lens would require a privately transferable lower-middle-market firm supplying recurring outsourced international-affairs services to external customers, while NAICS 928120 comprises establishments of U.S. and foreign governments.
lens.excluded_activities: U.S. and foreign ministries, embassies, consulates, diplomatic missions, foreign-assistance agencies, and other government establishments; private international-development contractors, policy consultancies, translation providers, travel processors, nonprofits, and software vendors classified elsewhere; captive units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Government establishments conduct diplomacy, consular affairs, international assistance, policy, negotiation, representation, analysis, public engagement, and overseas program administration under statutory authority, appropriations, and consular fees rather than commercial recurring-service contracts with external customers.

a.bridge: Department of State career descriptions identify the broader diplomatic task mix rather than wage-weighted hours for all establishments in NAICS 928120. I mapped research, reporting, document drafting, translation support, information retrieval, case triage, scheduling, routine public inquiries, program monitoring, and budget administration to exposure, while leaving negotiation, representation, relationship building, crisis response, security judgment, final adjudication, leadership, and accountable policy decisions unexposed. The mapping may overstate exposure where work uses classified or sensitive sources and understate it in high-volume consular and administrative units.
a.rationale: International-affairs work mixes information-heavy analysis and administration with irreducibly human diplomacy. AI can accelerate multilingual synthesis, first drafts, briefing preparation, trend detection, case support, public communications, and internal operations, but cannot assume sovereign authority or reliably replace trust, negotiation, contextual judgment, and crisis leadership.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-use study across State Department domestic bureaus, overseas posts, consular units, and foreign-assistance offices that distinguishes already automated work and measures hours by task, clearance level, language, and decision authority.
caveats: State Department roles are a proxy for the wider U.S. and foreign-government industry population.
Public sources omit many classified, sensitive, and national-security workflows.
Exposure is likely much higher in document and consular processing than in political engagement and negotiation.

rho.rationale: The State Department has established AI governance, inventories, use-case ownership, training, and risk-management processes, and broader federal use is expanding. Implementation remains constrained by classified and sensitive data, cybersecurity, diplomatic-source protection, records law, privacy and civil-liberties requirements, model reliability across languages, legacy systems, procurement, overseas connectivity, and extensive review chains.
rho.change_evidence: State Department AI inventory data and audited before-and-after deployments in cable drafting, research, translation, consular processing, grants, public inquiries, and program monitoring, including paid hours, cycle time, error rates, security incidents, and human-review intensity.
caveats: The public State inventory excludes or withholds national-security, defense, sensitive law-enforcement, and sensitive-but-unclassified uses.
Federal adoption evidence does not establish implementation rates for foreign governments.
Some adoption will expand analytical capacity or improve response time without reducing staffing.

e.rationale: The official NAICS definition limits this industry to establishments of U.S. and foreign governments. Such establishments are not privately transferable LMM firms supplying outsourced services to external customers, so the eligible share is zero under the fixed U.S. lens.
e.change_evidence: A formal Census NAICS revision admitting private commercial establishments to 928120, or authoritative record-level evidence that private firms are classified inside the code despite the published definition.
caveats: Private international-development, policy, technology, logistics, and language contractors may be acquisition candidates, but they are classified elsewhere.
The injected LMM firm-count input is missing because government establishments are outside the relevant firm datasets; it must not be replaced with contractor counts.

s5.rationale: A qualifying control-transfer probability is not defined because there is no eligible privately controlled firm population. Elections, diplomatic appointments, agency reorganizations, changes of government, and transfers of program authority are not acquisitions of operating companies.
s5.change_evidence: This primitive would become estimable only if authoritative classification evidence established a nonzero population of privately owned LMM firms inside NAICS 928120, followed by verified control-transfer histories for that population.
caveats: Zero was not imputed because there is no eligible population on which to define a transfer probability.
Outsourcing a diplomatic-support contract is not a transfer of the government establishment.

q.rationale: Commercial benefit retention is not defined for government international-affairs establishments. Operational savings can affect service levels, fee-funded accounts, staffing, or appropriations, but there is no private operator margin governed by customer pass-through and renewal pricing.
q.change_evidence: A valid in-industry population of private operators plus their customer contracts, fee structures, renewal pricing, procurement terms, and post-implementation margin data; evidence from contractors classified outside 928120 cannot fill this primitive.
caveats: Consular fees are statutory or administratively set public charges, not evidence of a transferable firm's commercial pricing power.
Budget savings may be redeployed or reduce future funding rather than accrue to an owner.

d5.bridge: State's passport series is a measured consular workload proxy: applications rose from 18,578,593 in fiscal 2019 to 20,437,207 in fiscal 2024, a five-year ratio of about 1.10. I temper that growth because passports are only one component of international affairs and because diplomatic, assistance, and policy workloads respond to legislation, conflicts, migration rules, travel cycles, and reorganizations. The proxy likely overstates broad demand if post-pandemic travel normalization drove the increase and understates it if cyber, AI, crisis, and great-power diplomacy add work faster than routine processing.
d5.rationale: International representation, citizen protection, secure travel, negotiation, economic and cyber diplomacy, and crisis coordination remain durable government functions. High consular volumes and newer technology-diplomacy responsibilities support modest growth centrally, while program cuts, agency consolidation, travel restrictions, or reduced foreign assistance create a meaningful downside case.
d5.change_evidence: Enacted five-year workload and operating plans across State and other international-affairs agencies covering passport and visa cases, citizen-assistance incidents, diplomatic posts, negotiations, grants, foreign-assistance programs, cyber and technology engagements, and appropriated staffing.
caveats: Passport applications are a narrow U.S. consular indicator, not a complete measure of international-affairs service quantity.
The 2019-to-2024 comparison spans pandemic disruption and recovery.
Foreign-government demand may diverge from U.S. workloads, and the estimate concerns constant-quality quantity rather than spending or employment.

o.rationale: Nearly all surviving demand requires an accountable government operator. Diplomatic recognition, negotiation, representation, consular adjudication, citizen protection, foreign-policy decisions, and official international commitments depend on sovereign authority even when software or contractors perform supporting analysis and processing.
o.change_evidence: Binding legal changes or operating evidence showing a material share of diplomatic, consular, foreign-assistance, or official-representation workload transferred entirely to self-service software or non-government entities without accountable agency control.
caveats: Digital self-service can reduce counter and case-processing work without removing governmental adjudication or accountability.
Agency consolidation changes organizational location rather than eliminating the operator requirement.

narrative.executive_view: International affairs contains meaningful AI-assistance potential in research, multilingual synthesis, document production, consular processing, program administration, and public service. It is nevertheless outside the roll-up lens: the industry consists of government establishments, the dataset has no receipts-based labor share or LMM firm count, and there is no privately transferable operator population.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can summarize large multilingual information flows, draft briefs and correspondence, support translation, identify patterns, triage routine cases, answer common questions, and assist grants, budgets, and reporting. Diplomats and accountable officials still must negotiate, represent sovereign positions, cultivate trust, protect sources, manage crises, and make legal and policy judgments.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits may improve timeliness, analytical reach, citizen service, and public-resource use, but they do not become commercial margin for an in-industry owner. Appropriations, fee-funded public accounts, oversight, and policy priorities determine disposition of savings.
narrative.firm_availability: There are no eligible firms under the authoritative industry definition. Acquisition opportunities among international-development contractors, consultancies, data providers, language-service firms, and diplomatic-support vendors must be evaluated in their own industries rather than imported into this record.
narrative.demand_durability: Diplomacy, consular protection, secure travel, international negotiation, and crisis coordination are durable state functions. Passport workload and expanding cyber and technology diplomacy suggest resilient quantity, while foreign-assistance cuts, travel-policy shifts, embassy changes, and government reorganizations can materially alter the service basket.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Task exposure is inferred from public roles, which omit classified and sensitive work. Security, privacy, source protection, multilingual reliability, records rules, procurement, and high-consequence decisions can slow implementation. The decisive weakness is structural rather than technical: sovereign government establishments cannot be acquired as LMM operating companies.
narrative.principal_driver: Large information flows and document-heavy analytical and consular workflows create credible AI-assistance opportunities while international state functions remain durable.
narrative.principal_weakness: The NAICS population is government-only and therefore contains no privately transferable LMM operators under the fixed lens.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: 928120 International Affairs
sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments of U.S. and foreign governments primarily engaged in international affairs and programs relating to other nations and peoples.

sources.title: Foreign Service Officer Career Tracks
sources.supports: Core diplomatic work across consular service, economic cooperation, embassy operations, political analysis and programs, public diplomacy, foreign-government engagement, and policy.

sources.title: 20 FAM 302.2: Compliance Standards for Artificial Intelligence
sources.supports: State Department AI governance, annual inventories, compliance certification, training, sensitive-use exclusions, rights and safety risk management, and accountable ownership requirements.

sources.title: Artificial Intelligence: Generative AI Use and Management at Federal Agencies
sources.supports: Generative AI's potential for internal operations and service delivery, rapid growth of federal use, and implementation challenges across selected agencies including the Department of State.

sources.title: Reports and Statistics: U.S. Passports
sources.supports: Annual passport application and issuance volumes, including 18,578,593 applications in fiscal 2019 and 20,437,207 in fiscal 2024, used as a consular-demand proxy.

sources.title: Cyber Diplomacy: State's Efforts Aim to Support U.S. Interests and Elevate Priorities
sources.supports: State's international cyber-diplomacy role, creation of a specialized bureau, and growing diplomatic activity around foreign cyber threats and emerging technology.
